data.title: Encouraging Growth In Key Metrics Drives 55% Gain Year-To-Date For Cloudflare Stock
data.description:
Cloudflare’s (NYSE:NET) stock rose 20% in the days following Q4 earnings with strong momentum for its Workers AI platform and accelerations in multiple key metrics, paving the way for the stock to potentially reaccelerate revenue growth while driving a shift to GAAP profitability. Management also expressed a high degree of optimism for AI inference and growth opportunities through 2025, especially for its Workers AI platform.

I break down the growth in the most important key metrics, Workers AI momentum and commentary about AI opportunities below.

Workers Developers Grew from 2 Million to 3 Million in 2024

Cloudflare has quietly amassed a large developer base with tens of millions of developers, allowing for a rather frictionless ramp for its Workers AI platform, with 2024 seeing the platform record more than 50% growth. Workers AI positions Cloudflare to capitalize on the growing AI inference market via its unique approach that offers performance at or exceeding hyperscalers’ at a lower cost.

In Q4, management noted that active Workers developers crossed 3 million, marking a 50% growth from 2 million active developers in Q1 and a 25% growth from 2.4 million developers in Q2. Since November 2023, active Workers developers have risen more than 200%.

The Workers platform essentially is Cloudflare’s moat and what separates it from the rest of software in terms of what it can provide for AI. With Workers, Cloudflare eliminates cold starts, allowing code to be executed the moment it's received – we had explained to our premium members in 2023 that it's a "combination of competing with the hyperscalers, delivering app performance at faster speeds... while keeping prices low... that's unique to Cloudflare," and what will help the platform drive growth down the road.

Management hammered home the significance of Workers in Q4’s earnings call, emphasizing that the:

Killer application for Cloudflare Workers is turning out to be AI. The model of programming is uniquely suited for building tools like AI agents, and our serverless architecture, which allows you to pay only for what you use based on CPU or GPU type, positions Workers to become the go-to platform for developers who want the best price performance for AI inference and agentic workflows.”

Cloudflare is now capitalizing on the developer moat that it has built with Workers over the past few years, as developers continue to choose the platform due to the unparalleled efficiencies and cost advantages it can offer when it comes to AI inference. Customers executing inference tasks could pay up to 250% more with hyperscalers than with Cloudflare’s pay-per-use model, per management.

Workers also saw significant deal momentum in the fourth quarter. Cloudflare reported its largest customer win in Q4, a $20 million contract with a Fortune 1000 tech firm, that includes Workers and application security, while a leading AI firm expanded with a $13.5 million deal for services including Workers, R2, and more.

What could easily be overlooked (and what arguably is very important) was management’s discussion on GPU utilization rates. Management explained that while a typical customer at a hyperscaler “is seeing sub-10% utilization across their GPUs, our peak utilization of GPUs is now around 70%,” though troughs are much lower with room to improve in 2025. What this means is that Cloudflare “can get effectively 7 times today the amount of work out of $1 of capex spent,” allowing them to capture the excess either in margins or pass it on to customers via lower costs. Improving troughs in utilization should theoretically lead to faster processing times and an ability to handle more requests for customers, all while doing so for cheaper and at a higher margin.

Multiple Key Metrics Support Confidence in Cloudflare’s Revenue Reacceleration

Though Workers growth and commentary on improving GPU utilization rates were quiet drivers of the report, multiple key metrics showed growth in Q4, supporting management’s confidence that the first half of 2025 would mark a turning point with the strength of its business accelerating in the second half.

Cloudflare guided for 23.7% growth in Q1 to $468-469 million in revenue, and that's currently forecast to be the bottom for the year, with revenue expected to reaccelerate to the 27% level exiting the year. All it would take is a few beat-and-raise quarters to put Cloudflare on track to surpass the 30% growth level by the end of 2025.

Multiple key metrics showed strong growth in Q4, with a handful of analysts praising the productivity strides and strong execution on these key metrics in the quarter:

  • Acceleration in growth in >$1M customers
  • High concentration of net new adds for >$1M customers in Q4
  • Acceleration in paying customers
  • Acceleration in billings and strong Pool of Funds activity
  • DBNRR expanding

Cloudflare’s Paying Customer Growth Accelerates

Cloudflare reported 173 customers in the >$1M cohort at the end of 2024, for 47% YoY growth, up from 37% growth last year. Management noted that of the 55 of the net new adds in 2024, more than half came in Q4, implying at least 28 in the quarter, and 27 combined for the other three quarters; a significant uptick.

Paying customers increased 25% YoY to 237,714 at the end of Q4, an 8-point acceleration from the start of the year. From late 2022 through 2023, growth had remained in the mid-teens, with the recent uptick in paying customers coming alongside growth in AI applications and more mainstream AI usage, with numerous consumer-facing and enterprise-focused models being released. What’s impressive is that Cloudflare is matching 2022 growth rates at a nearly 100,000 larger paying customer scale. Additionally, two of the past three quarters have seen paying customers grow at 7% QoQ, suggesting that a further acceleration could be still in store.

Cloudflare’s Billings Growth Tops 30% Again

Billings meaningfully inflected in Q4, rising nearly 32% YoY and 22% QoQ to $548 million. Growth had previously been in the low-20% range for the first three quarters of 2024. Billings likely benefited from a “notable uptick in close rates” and “an improvement in sales cycles,” with management mentioning that the majority of large customers “whose deals had slipped from Q3, reengaged and signed significant contracts in Q4.”

Pool of Funds activity was quite strong as well, with activity comparable to Q3 at around 9 percentage points. Management noted that a $20 million Pool of Funds contract with a Fortune 100 tech company was the largest new customer win in company history, along with a $13.5 million deal with a leading AI firm. Pool of Funds is expected to pick up strength in the second half of the year after leading to some near-term headwinds as customers transition to these contracts.

There were other encouraging signs within ramped account executives, with management saying that they:

Delivered a 10 percentage point increase in ramped AEs achieving over 80% of quota compared with 2023, with most gains coming in the 125% or higher attainment cohorts.” For 2025, management is expecting YoY growth in ramped account executives to “accelerate each quarter throughout 2025, further laying the foundation for Cloudflare's next phase of growth at scale.”

DBNRR Ticks Higher to 111%

Cloudflare reported that its dollar-based net retention rate (DBNRR) ticked 1 point higher to 111%, marking the first time the metric has grown since Q3 2023. Management commented that:

There can be some variability in this metric quarter-to-quarter, but we believe the recent decelerating trend in DNR is stabilizing despite continued near-term headwinds from increased traction with pool of fund contracts.

Cloudflare’s GPU Investments Rising

With the accelerations in billings, paying customers and strong net adds in its largest customer cohort, Cloudflare laid the foundation for increased investments to support growth through 2025. While this is likely to be slightly detrimental to margins, with management guided for an adjusted operating margin of 11.6% in Q1 versus 14.6% in Q2, it paves the way for Cloudflare to meet higher demand and reinvigorate revenue growth.

Management said that the “accelerating shift from AI training to AI inference has given us confidence to continue to increase our investment in our GPU rollout as we provision greater capacity to support demand in 2025.” As a result, network capex is expected to be 12-13% of revenue in 2025, up from 10% in 2024.

As has been the case with hyperscalers, who had been quite vocal over the past few quarters that demand regularly outpaced GPU supply, Cloudflare signaled that its investments in GPU capacity would go toward supporting higher demand as workloads shift toward AI inference.

Cloudflare is Inching Closer to GAAP Profitability

Breaking into GAAP profitability on the bottom line is a "holy grail" for cloud stocks in part due to high SBC levels that push operating expenses above 100% of revenue. Cloudflare is inching closer to reaching GAAP profitability on the bottom, though GAAP operating income is a bit further away.

Cloudflare reported a GAAP operating loss of ($34.7 million), or a GAAP operating margin of -7.5% in Q4. Though this declined slightly from -7.2% in Q3 due to some headwinds to gross margin, it marked a 7-point improvement from the -14.4% operating margin in Q1.

Cloudflare is showing prudent cost management, bringing expenses down from 92% of revenue in Q1 to below 84% of revenue in Q4. R&D expenses ticked slightly higher sequentially in Q4, understandable given the investments Cloudflare is making in its network, while marketing expenses declined 1.4 points to 41.7% of revenue.

GAAP net margin is making strides toward positive territory, with Cloudflare benefiting from net interest income and improvements to operating margin. GAAP net margin improved from -9.4% in Q1 to -2.8% in Q4, or a loss of just ($12.8 million). This improvement comes despite SBC remaining elevated at just above 20% of revenue.

Cloudflare is currently not expected to break into GAAP profitability this year, with analysts expecting a loss each quarter of 2025. However, the improvements in operating margin from cost management efforts and a potential revenue reacceleration could shift that story. Other best-of-breed cloud names such as CrowdStrike and DataDog have seen strong returns after breaking into GAAP profitability on the bottom line.

Cloudflare’s Valuation Looks Elevated

Even though management is quite confident in the AI inference growth opportunities in 2025, there’s not yet tangible evidence of a reacceleration on the top line, and Cloudflare is trading at its highest valuation in more than two years. Key metrics are showing underlying signs that support revenue reaccelerating, but the valuation amplifies risks to the downside until the acceleration story materializes.

On the top line, Cloudflare is trading at its highest valuations since May 2022, at 28x forward revenue, above where it had previously found resistance at 22x to 23x forward revenue. This makes it one of the most expensive cloud stocks aside from Palantir, trading at a near 100% premium to DataDog and Snowflake, despite all three reporting revenue growth in the high 26% to 28% range for the most recent quarter.

On a free cash flow basis, Cloudflare is the most expensive of the four here, trading at 350x free cash flow versus 250x for Palantir and 75x to 77x for Snowflake and DataDog. Free cash flow has hovered at ~10% of revenue for 2024, below Snowflake at 20% through fiscal Q3 and DataDog at 29% for 2024 due to Cloudflare’s much higher investments in its infrastructure – Cloudflare spent $185 million in 2024, vs. $35 million for DataDog in 2024 and $34 million for Snowflake (through Q3).

For adjusted EPS, Cloudflare is valued at 217x forward earnings of $0.80, vs. 210x for Palantir and 186x for Snowflake. This is a significant re-rating in a short period of time, with Cloudflare being valued at half of this multiple in October, at 105x forward earnings. Adjusted earnings growth is also expected to be minimal in 2025, with management guiding for barely 6% growth to $0.79 to $0.80 this year.

Conclusion

The Workers platform exhibited strong momentum through 2024 with active developers rising 50% to surpass 3 million, widening Cloudflare’s developer moat as it aims to harness growth in AI via its positioning at the edge and ability to offer high-performance, low-cost inference. A handful of key metrics inflected and accelerated in unison in Q4, a positive sign for growth heading into 2025.

Cloudflare’s management expressed confidence for 2025 on AI inference-driven opportunities, with multiple key metrics suggesting that a revenue reacceleration could be in store. Given the soft Q1 and fiscal 2025 guide and elevated valuation, Cloudflare is in a spot where it has to prove that it can meaningfully inflect revenue growth based on the underlying strength in large customer deals, billings, and customer growth. We think the stock will prove this; yet we also book gains at specific price levels to reduce risk.

Damien Robbins, Equity Analyst for the Tech Insider Network, contributed to this analysis.

Disclaimer: This is not financial advice. Please consult with your financial advisor in regards to any stocks you buy.

Recommended Reading:

Palantir Stock Sets Path Towards 40% Growth

DeepSeek Creates Buying Opportunity For Nvidia Stock

5 Top Tech Stocks Of 2024: Year In Review

This Is Not Broadcom's 'Nvidia Moment' Yet

data.title: Gold Eyes Fresh Highs, U.S.-Russia Talks Fail To Quell Haven Demand
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Risk aversion persisted in the markets today, even as a U.S.-Russia meeting in Saudi Arabia sparked hopes for a potential decline in safe-haven demand. Additionally, reports emerged of a phase two agreement being reached between Israel and Hamas.

Both developments carried the expectation of easing the geopolitical tensions that have weighed on global sentiment over the past 12 to 18 months. However, these events have so far failed to significantly alleviate the sense of geopolitical risk, keeping haven demand firmly in place.

US-Russia Talks in Saudi Arabia

A US and Russian delegation met in Saudi Arabia today to discuss a potential end to the Ukraine conflict. Market participants may have been eyeing a drop in geopolitics, which may lead to a drop in Gold prices as haven demand falls. However, this failed to materialize despite reports that the talks were positive.

Key Quotes and Reports from the Meeting from Both US and Russian Officials:

-US, Russia Agree To Appoint Teams To End Ukraine Conflict – US Official

-US Spox Bruce: One Call One Meeting Not Enough For Enduring Peace

-Russia’s Dmitriev: Russian, US Officials Have Separate Discussion On Future Economic Cooperation, Including Global Energy Prices – RTRS

-US, Russia Peace Proposal Would Include Ukraine Elections – Fox

-US State Sec Rubio: US, Russia Agree To Restore Embassy Staffing -AP

-U.S. National Security Adviser Waltz: There Will Be Talks About Territory And Security Guarantees

-US Sec Of State Rubio: Everyone Involved In Conflict Has To Be Part Of Talks

-US Sec Of State Rubio: European Union Needs To Be Involved At Some Point

-US National Security Adviser Waltz: US Allies Are Being Consulted On Ukraine

-US National Security Adviser Waltz: US Is Glad Europe Is Talking About Contributing More Forcefully To Ukraine Security

-Russia’s ForMin Lavrov: We Agreed To Form Process For Ukraine Conflict Settlement

-Russia’s ForMin Lavrov: There Were Also High Interest To Lift Barriers For Economic Cooperation

-Russia’s ForMin Lavrov: European Troops In Ukraine Is Unacceptable For Russia

-Russia’s ForMin Lavrov: I Can Say That Today’s Talks Were Not Unsuccessful

Despite the comments by Russia’s Foreign Minister Lavrov that the talks were not unsuccessful, the road ahead may be a long and bumpy one. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy announced that his visit to Saudi Arabia has been rescheduled for March 10th, as he awaits the arrival of the U.S. delegation in Kyiv. This could explain why markets have not had any meaningful reaction as of yet.

Israel-Hamas Phase Two Deal Appears on Course

In another positive for risk sentiment, both sides appear ready to discuss phase two of the ceasefire. Israeli Foreign Minister Gideon Saar stated that negotiations for the second phase of the hostage deal will commence soon. This view was echoed by Hamas Gaza Chief Hayya, who announced that the group is prepared to begin immediate negotiations for the second phase of the Gaza ceasefire agreement.

Another potential positive for geopolitical risk. Israeli Foreign Minister meanwhile acknowledged he is aware that Arab states are coming up with a plan for Gaza as Egyptian President Sisi is set to travel to Saudi Arabia for discussions. On the flip side however, the Israeli Foreign Minister has said they are unwilling to accept or support a plan that would see civilian control of Gaza transferred from Hamas to the Palestinian Authority.

Tariff Developments and the Week Ahead

The Dollar has so far had a limited impact on Gold prices. We have seen spikes when key data has missed estimates but the precious metal quickly recovers. This is a sign that haven demand and risk sentiment remains skewed in favor of further gains for Gold prices.

Tariffs by the US have heated up as well which continues to support Gold prices. President Trump has said that tariffs on automobiles would come on April 2, while Chinese lithium company Jiangsu Jiuwu Hi-Tech halted exports of processing equipment per LSEG.

Signs that a global trade war remains a massive concern for market participants moving forward.

On the data front, the FOMC minutes will be out on Wednesday followed by S&P PMI data on Friday. Both of these events could lead to a spike in volatility, but I do expect any moves to prove short-lived.

Technical Analysis – Gold (XAU/USD)

From a technical analysis standpoint, this analysis is a follow up from the technicals last week. Read: Gold (XAU/USD) Outlook: $3000/oz Target Possible as Safe Haven Demand Rises

Gold prices did experience a pullback last week following hotter than expected inflation data from the US. However, this proved short-lived as prices retested its alltime highs around the 2942/0z handle.

On the daily timeframe, the RSI period 14 is back in overbought territory. As we know though, an instrument can remain in overbought territory for extended periods of time.

Gold (XAU/USD) Daily Chart, February 18, 2025

Dropping down to a four-hour chart H4, markets are in a sort of range with highs around 2937 and lows around 2881.

A rejection at 3937 could lead to a retest of support at 2924 and 2913 before the 2900 comes into focus.

On the upside, a break of 2927 and the all-time high at 2942 will see focus shift toward the 2950 and 2975 resistance handles.

Gold (XAU/USD) Four-Hour H4 Chart, February 18, 2025

Support

  • 2924
  • 2013
  • 2900

Resistance

  • 2937
  • 2942
  • 2950

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: REIT Earnings Halftime Report
data.description:
REIT Earnings Halftime Report

We're at the halfway point of another consequential real estate earnings season, with 60 of the roughly 150 equity REITs and 18 of the 38 mortgage REITs representing 50% of the total market capitalization now having reported results. Buoyed by much-needed relief on the interest rate front, REIT earnings season is off to a strong start both fundamentally and performance-wise. Of the 51 equity REITs that provide full-year guidance for Funds from Operations ("FFO"), 31 (61%) beat their most recent full-year outlook, while just three (6%) missed their outlook - above the historical average "beat rate" for the fourth quarter of roughly 55%. Strong trends within the real estate sector come alongside a similarly solid start to earnings season across the broader U.S. equity market. FactSet reports that 76% of the 385 S&P 500 constituents that have reported results beat consensus Earnings Per Share ("EPS") estimates, which is slightly above the 10-year average of 75%.

Overarching trends have generally been consistent with the "green shoots" identified in our REIT Earnings Preview. As expected, we've observed a post-election rebound in logistics demand, a continued recovery of office leasing, and sluggish residential rent growth amid peaking multifamily supply. Healthcare REITs have been the "star of the show" so far this earnings season, as senior housing results showed robust - and continuing - momentum in senior housing operating fundamentals. Retail REITs have also been upside surprises thus far, with strip center and mall REITs reporting another quarter of near-record occupancy rates and rent growth, while pushing back on concern over a sudden surge in retail bankruptcies. Results from Net Lease and Residential Mortgage REITs - two of the most rate-sensitive sectors - have been surprisingly steady despite the turbulence in late 2024. Office REIT results have been mildly disappointing, as an encouraging property-level recovery has been stymied by higher financing costs. Relatedly, Commercial Mortgage REITs have disappointed so far after another flurry of office loan defaults and a stubborn uptick in sector-wide delinquency rates, underscoring the bifurcation between property-level trends and bottom-line profitability.

We've seen 20 REITs raise their dividends since the start of earnings season - slightly ahead of last year's pacing at this time, boosted by the first dividend hikes in nearly a decade for a pair of major healthcare REITs: Senior housing REIT Ventas (VTR) raised its dividend by 7% (2.9% dividend yield) - its first dividend hike since 2018. Medical office REIT Healthpeak (DOC) raised its dividend by 2% (6.2% dividend yield) - its first dividend increase since 2016. Other highlights include a hearty 20% increase from First Industrial (FR), a 15% increase from single-family rental REIT American Homes (AMH), a 12% increase from strip center REIT Urban Edge (UE), a pair of 10% hikes from Equinix (EQIX) and Iron Mountain (IRM), and a healthy 8% dividend hike from manufactured housing REIT Equity Lifestyle (ELS). That said, we also saw the first REIT dividend reduction of the year last week, with commercial lender Ares Commercial (ACRE) slashing its payout by 40% after reporting ongoing distress in its office and life sciences-backed loans. Below, we award each property sector a Halftime Grade - ranked in order from best to worst - and highlight quick incremental positives and negatives we've observed.

Healthcare REIT Halftime Report

Healthcare: (Halftime Grade: A-)

  • Positives: Stellar senior housing trends continue in 2025, healthier skilled nursing operators, VTR & DOC dividend increases.
  • Negatives: Status quo for medical office, lethargic lab space fundamentals, lingering SNF policy uncertainty

Regional Mall Earnings

Mall: (Halftime Grade: B+)

  • Positives: Strong leasing despite retailer bankruptcies, Simon's dividend hike to pre-pandemic levels, positive FFO outlook for 2025
  • Negatives: Flat tenant sales & foot traffic, Concern on spending health of European & lower-end American consumer

Net Lease REIT Halftime Report

Net Lease: (Halftime Grade: B+)

  • Positives: Perfect slate of FFO beats, healthy but disciplined acquisition activity, WPC's return to growth, positive commentary on retail credit
  • Negatives: Modest cap rate compression, additional retail categories join the watch list (car washes, following Zips bankruptcy)

Data Center REIT Halftime Report

Data Center: (Halftime Grade: B)

  • Positives: EQIX's record bookings in Q4, DLR's long-awaited return to FFO growth, upbeat DeepSeek and AI commentary
  • Negatives: Softer bookings for DLR, moderating (but still solid) pricing power after record 2024, IRM's disappointing 2025 bookings outlook

Strip Center REIT Halftime Report

Strip Centers: (Halftime Grade: B)

  • Positives: Solid 2025 outlook implying FFO growth near 5%, little slowdown in leasing activity, Renewal spreads at fresh record-highs
  • Negatives: Uptick in tenant bankruptcies, less bullish retail commentary, some "mean-reversion" from KRG after sector-leading pandemic growth

Industrial REIT Halftime Report

Industrial: (Halftime Grade: B)

  • Positives: Post-election demand rebound, encouraging 2025 outlook for moderating supply growth by mid-year, strength in Sunbelt markets
  • Negatives: Ongoing weakness in Southern California, market rents remain negative, moderating rent spreads after record-setting increases

Office REIT Halftime Report

Office: (Halftime Grade: B)

  • Positives: Leasing activity recovers to pre-pandemic average, Sunbelt & NYC strength, CUZ remains "unicorn" among office REITs
  • Negatives: Disappointing 2025 FFO outlook on higher interest rate expense (BDN in particular), DOGE concerns hit government office

Apartment REIT Halftime Report

Apartment: (Halftime Grade: B)

  • Positives: Buoyant renewal rent growth, record-low turnover helping to control expenses, healthy occupancy despite record supply growth
  • Negatives: Weak new lease rates amid peaking multifamily supply, FFO growth resumes in 2026, limited appetite for external growth

Mortgage REIT Halftime Report

Residential mREITs: (Halftime Grade: B+)

  • Positives: Decent results given harsh macro backdrop, positive dividend commentary, upbeat outlook on return on equity potential
  • Negatives: Results from CIM underscore the potential Book Value impact of volatile interest rates (but no permanent damage in this case)

Commercial mREITs: (Halftime Grade: C+)

  • Positives: Multifamily improvement, BXMT's "positive inflection" and improved loan performance, limited issues outside of office
  • Negatives: Poor outcomes on office resolutions, ACRE's additional office write-off and life science downgrade, GPMT's student housing downgrade

Previewing The Second-Half of Earnings

Buoyed by much-needed relief on the interest rate front, REIT earnings season is off to a strong start both fundamentally and performance-wise - but there's still much to uncover, with results still outstanding from the entire hotel, self-storage, billboard, cannabis, farmland, and cold storage sectors, along with the majority of smaller-cap REITs and several "embattled" names including hospital owner Medical Properties Trust (MPW). As discussed above, Senior Housing REITs have been the "star of the show" so far this earnings season, while Retail REITs have also been upside surprises. Commercial Mortgage REITs have disappointed so far, underscoring the bifurcation between property-level trends and bottom-line profitability resulting from financing challenges. We'll continue to provide real-time coverage and follow-up analysis articles in our Daily Recap and Weekly Outlook reports.

For an in-depth analysis of all real estate sectors, check out all of our quarterly reports: Apartments, Homebuilders, Manufactured Housing, Student Housing, Single-Family Rentals, Cell Towers, Casinos, Industrial, Data Center, Malls, Healthcare, Net Lease, Shopping Centers, Hotels, Billboards, Office, Farmland, Storage, Timber, Mortgage, and Cannabis.

Disclosure: Hoya Capital Real Estate advises two Exchange-Traded Funds listed on the NYSE. In addition to any long positions listed below, Hoya Capital is long all components in the Hoya Capital Housing 100 Index and in the Hoya Capital High Dividend Yield Index. Index definitions and a complete list of holdings are available on our website.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Momentum Leaves Other Equity Factors In The Dust So Far In 2025
data.description:
The momentum factor is crushing the competition year to date, based on a set of ETF proxies through Friday’s close (Feb. 14). After a strong run last year, momentum is roaring higher in 2025 in relative and absolute terms.

The iShares MSCI USA Momentum Factor ETF (MTUM) has surged 10.3% this year, more than twice the gain for the broad equity market benchmark via SPDR S&P 500 (SPY). The next-best factor is also well behind MTUM’s bull run so far in 2025: An ETF tracking the high-beta factor (SPHB) is in a distant second-place with a 5.4% rise this year.

Momentum’s strong leadership is all the more striking for a year so far that reflects a rising tide that’s lifted all factor boats. But while everything is posting gains, the laggards are far behind the leaders. The weakest performer so far this year: small-cap value (IJS), which is barely hanging on to a razor-thin advance of just 0.3% year to date.

What’s driving MTUM’s bull run? The fund’s top-two holdings are a key part of its success so far this year. MTUM’s top holding – semiconductor firm Broadcom (AVGO), with a 6.2% portfolio weight, according Morningstar.com - is up 16% so far in 2025. The second-largest holding is Walmart (WMT) with a 5.4% weight and a 15.2% year to date gain.

By comparison, the two largest holdings in the market benchmark – SPDR S&P 500 (SPY) – are posting sharply softer results: Apple (AAPL) is down 2.2% year to date while Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) is up a modest 3.4%.

The momentum factor, in short, is killing it so far in 2025.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: 4D Molecular Therapeutics: A Smart Bet On Long-Term VEGF Suppression
data.description:
Although the gene therapy space has seen its share of hype cycles, 4D Molecular Therapeutics (NASDAQ:FDMT) remains an undervalued player with a strategic edge. Often investors lump it in with broader biotech volatility and miss the company's unique platform - and the way its lead candidate, 4D-150, is quietly positioning itself as a category-defining asset in retinal disease. Clinical progress recently suggests that the market underestimates FDMT's ability to disrupt the existing anti-VEGF treatment paradigm. A pivotal trial is imminent, FDA alignment is strong, and there is a good chance of winning a large market share in the wet AMD and DME markets - even with skepticism about gene therapy.

Financial Situation

4D Molecular Therapeutics had a $551 million cash position as of September 30, 2024, and management expects this to fund operations through the first half of 2027. The company has a quarterly burn rate of about $46 million based on current spending trends. Evidently, it remains in the red with a $43.8 million net loss in Q3 2024 - not unusual for a clinical-stage biotech in active development. What is notable is that despite rising R&D spending - $38.5 million vs $25.1 million in Q3 - the balance sheet is healthy, and FDMT has roughly 12 full quarters of runway before a cash raise is needed.

Such financial stability is critical in the current biotech funding environment, where many small-cap peers are cash strapped and forced to cut programs or sell shares at low valuations. This gives 4DMT an advantage because it can fund multiple late-stage trials without an immediate financing overhang. It also suggests that the company will keep control of its development timeline, rather than jumping into partnerships that may not be long-term best for the company.

A concern for investors is the growing costs - notably in general and administrative expenses, which grew from $9.1 million to $12.7 million year-on-year. Some of that is to be expected as the company scales operations ahead of pivotal trials, but it raises questions about long-term cost discipline. However, with FDMT now entering Phase 3 for 4D-150, the increased expenditures may represent an upfront investment in commercialization infrastructure rather than uncontrolled burn. In a market where biotech capital is becoming more expensive, the company's ability to keep its cash runway intact well into 2027 without dilution will likely be a key differentiator if 4D-150 goes as expected.

Development Pipeline

The pipeline is based on the company's proprietary AAV vector platform, but the real driver is 4D-150, its gene therapy candidate for wet AMD and diabetic macular edema. Its Phase 2 data indicates that fewer supplemental injections are needed compared to the standard aflibercept treatment - an outcome that may change the management of these diseases. The implications are not that 4D-150 is a new player in the anti-VEGF crowded space, but a fundamentally different approach that could set a new standard of care by addressing patient adherence.

Beyond 4D-150, FDMT has a second ophthalmology asset, 4D-175, which targets geographic atrophy via complement inhibition. Recent FDA approvals draw attention to the GA therapies market, but competition is fierce. Its potential advantage is that 4D-175 uses gene therapy and thus could have a longer-lasting action than repeated injections of complement inhibitors.

Its pipeline extends outside ophthalmology to include 4D-710 for cystic fibrosis patients ineligible for CFTR modulator therapy. This program is riskier because of the inherent challenges that lung-directed gene therapy has, but the addressable market is large. But unlike the ophthalmology assets, 4D-710 is more scientifically uncertain because pulmonary delivery of gene therapy has historically been a difficult problem to solve. But its approach - aerosolized single-dose delivery - suggests the company is being pragmatic rather than overpromising on a high-dose systemic approach.

Drug Mechanisms and Science

Wet AMD and diabetic macular edema are caused by excessive VEGF signaling, which causes abnormal blood vessel growth and leakage in the retina, leading to vision impairment. Anti-VEGF injections like aflibercept have been the gold standard of care for years but require frequent administration, leading to treatment gaps and disease progression due to non-adherence. To circumvent this problem, 4D-150 delivers genetic instructions for the continuous expression of two anti-VEGF transgenes in retinal cells via gene therapy using a proprietary R100 vector. Contrary to conventional bolus injections which cause fluctuating VEGF suppression, this method allows steady, localized inhibition and may maintain therapeutic levels for years with a single dose.

Above and beyond VEGF, 4D-175 targets another mechanism of geographic atrophy, an advanced form of dry AMD characterized by the progressive destruction of retinal cells. Unlike wet AMD, GA is associated with chronic dysregulation of the complement system - an immune system involved in inflammation and cell clearance. Excessive complement activation causes irreversible vision loss by damaging retinal pigment epithelial cells. The 4D-175 transgene encodes a shortened form of complement factor H, a key regulator of complement activity, to restore balance and possibly slow disease progression. This approach has strong rationale - current complement inhibitors require ongoing intravitreal injections to maintain efficacy, whereas gene therapy may provide a more sustained and stable level of complement control without the need for repeated dosing.

The company's efforts with 4D-710 in cystic fibrosis represent a different challenge. Unlike its ophthalmology programs, which offer locally tailored treatment in an immune-privileged environment, gene therapy for cystic fibrosis must overcome the lung's natural defenses such as mucus barriers and immune responses that prevent vector penetration. Using the A101 vector, 4D-710 is delivered as an aerosolized therapy to airway epithelial cells for expression of a functional CFTR∆R transgene. The novelty here is its mutation-agnostic design. Currently, available CF treatments are dependent on specific genetic mutations, so a subset of patients has no treatment options. If 4D-710 can achieve robust lung transduction and durable CFTR expression, it may fill a critical unmet need in patients not responding to existing modulator therapies.

Regulatory Clarity and Early DME Data for 4D-150

This update from 4D Molecular Therapeutics on 4D-150 had two key takeaways: the drug still shows strong clinical activity for diabetic macular edema, and the FDA has aligned with the company on a streamlined regulatory pathway. Both developments reduce uncertainty regarding the drug's future and reinforce its potential to challenge the current anti-VEGF treatment paradigm. The Phase 2 SPECTRA trial demonstrated that patients receiving the highest dose of 4D-150 (3E10 vg/eye) required fewer supplemental aflibercept injections, and more than half were injection-free through 32 weeks.

Arguably more significant than the clinical data itself is the regulatory clarity provided by the update. The FDA has indicated that the results of one Phase 3 trial, together with data from the two planned Phase 3 trials in wet AMD, will be sufficient for a Biologics License Application submission. Hence, 4DMT does not need to initiate a separate Phase 2b study (SPECTRA Part 2), and can enter Phase 3 directly. Consolidating regulatory requirements for its wet AMD and DME programs suggests that 4D-150 is being considered a therapy of choice rather than a niche gene therapy asset.

I think it’s important to emphasize that 4D-150's mechanism of action is unique among conventional anti-VEGF therapies. Rather than targeting just one VEGF family member, the therapy suppresses four different VEGF isoforms at the site of disease activity, which may explain the long-term durability. So far, data shows that even with conservative dose selection, 4D-150 is providing clinically meaningful visual and retinal thickness improvements, demonstrating its long-term promise.

4D-150’s Recent Data Supports Sustained Efficacy in Wet AMD

The latest 52-week interim data from the PRISM Phase 2b trial further solidifies 4D-150's potential to shake up the treatment landscape for wet AMD. In patients receiving the planned 3E10 vg/eye dose, supplemental aflibercept injections were reduced by 83% from standard treatment expectations, and over half remained injection-free for a year. Results substantiate the therapy's durability while addressing the biggest problem with current anti-VEGF therapies: the necessity of frequent injections. These data also suggest a dose-response effect, with the subgroup of recently diagnosed patients benefiting from receiving 94% fewer injections and remaining injection-free. Hence, earlier intervention with 4D-150 may lead to better long-term outcomes and potentially change prescribing behaviors if approved.

Besides the reduction in treatment burden, 4D-150 showed stable vision preservation with + 2.2 letters best corrected visual acuity in all patients and + 3.1 letters in the recently diagnosed subgroup. Such stability is important because earlier long-term anti-VEGF therapies sacrificed durability for efficacy. No safety has worsened with longer follow-up, and adverse events have been limited to mild, transient intraocular inflammation in only two of 71 patients, both of whom recovered fully. This contrasts with some earlier gene therapy candidates in ophthalmology, which failed because of dose-limiting inflammation.

The most overlooked part of this update was the demonstration that aflibercept expression in the aqueous humor was stable for up to two years, supporting the long-term viability of 4D-150's gene therapy approach. Investors tend to think of gene therapies as a high-risk, binary outcome proposition, but this data set suggests 4D-150 is delivering on its promise of sustained VEGF suppression with a single intravitreal injection. With upcoming Phase 3 trials (4FRONT-1 and 4FRONT-2) starting in 2025, the therapy is on a clear regulatory path.

Risks and Potential Counterarguments

Despite the promising data 4D-150 has generated, its approach is not without risks, both clinically and commercially. The most immediate question is whether the durability observed in early trials will translate to maintained long-term efficacy in larger, more diverse patient populations. In theory, gene therapy is superior in that it can sustain therapeutic benefit, but durability in practice is variable. Some gene therapies were initially efficacious but transgene expression declined over time, requiring additional interventions. If 4D-150 does not sustain VEGF suppression beyond the observed two years, it may be perceived as an expensive and complicated alternative instead of a paradigm shift. The company will need robust durability data over longer follow-up periods to establish its position as a long-term solution.

But scientifically, some experts question whether sustained VEGF suppression is the best long-term strategy for retinal diseases. The VEGF pathway is a normal part of vascular function, and prolonged suppression may have undesirable consequences, such as atrophy worsening in patients already at risk for geographic atrophy. This remains an unresolved debate in the field, and although 4D-150 data so far does not support increased atrophy, longer trials are needed to assess this risk. Some ophthalmologists also suggest that some dosing flexibility must be maintained because disease progression varies widely between patients. Changing treatment levels after administration is much more involved than changing the injection frequency, which may be a limitation for clinicians who would like a more flexible treatment approach.

The big commercial hurdles will be pricing and reimbursement. Gene therapies often have high upfront costs that could make payers reluctant to cover them, unless they can demonstrate an economic benefit over repeated aflibercept injections. Reduced injection burden is a compelling value proposition, but insurers may push back on whether the cost savings are meaningful enough to justify a change in the standard of care. Moreover, if in the next few years new extended-dosing biologics or small-molecule inhibitors enter the landscape, 4D-150 might lose its competitive advantage long before it becomes widely available.

Valuation and TAM

Currently, 4D Molecular Therapeutics market capitalization is approximately $220 million. Its valuation is modest when compared to the vast market potential of its lead candidate, 4D-150, which targets wet age-related macular degeneration and diabetic macular edema.

Global macular edema and macular degeneration markets were valued at $10.33 billion in 2023 and are expected to reach around $22.51 billion by 2034 at a compound annual growth rate of 7.34%. Wet AMD is not that rare - in the United States alone, it affects about 1.75 million people. Current standard therapies, such as aflibercept (EYLEA), involve frequent intravitreal injections and have high annual costs. For example, there is an estimated $20,349 total treatment cost of EYLEA HD for wet AMD, DME, or diabetic retinopathy in a Q8W dosing regimen after 3 initial monthly doses during the first year.

The financial implications are significant if 4D-150 is approved by regulators and takes even a small share of this market. At $20,000 per treatment and 10% market penetration across the U.S. wet AMD population (roughly 175,000 patients), this would translate to potential revenues of $3.5 billion if priced competitively. That figure doesn't include additional opportunities in DME or potential expansion into other markets.

Conventional metrics are unlikely to make sense for clinical-stage biotechs, but the science and recent supportive data releases are hard to ignore. Given that 4D-150 can maintain efficacy with minimal treatment burden, it represents a strong value proposition over existing therapies in a large and rapidly growing market.

Takeaways

In my opinion, 4D molecular therapeutics is a strong case in the gene therapy space - one where the risk-reward profile is clearly biased towards upside potential. What the company has done is succeed in creating what many of its biotech peers struggle with: a clear regulatory path, a strong balance sheet providing a runway into 2027, and a lead program delivering differentiated clinical results that meet an immediate unmet need. Based on solid science, 4D-150 exploits targeted gene expression to overcome challenges inherent to anti-VEGF treatments for decades. Continuing to demonstrate durable efficacy without safety trade-offs could reshape the standard of care for both wet AMD and DME and secure a place in a billion-dollar market annually.

Despite this, the market is hesitant, perhaps reflecting broader skepticism about gene therapy and a longing for more conventional treatment modalities. Still, the valuation disconnect is stark. Even a modest share of the retinal disease market, especially when compared to the pricing power that long-acting, high-impact therapies typically command, is far greater than the market capitalization of FDMT. While execution risks remain - particularly in terms of long-term durability and commercialization strategy - the key ingredients for success are in place.

data.title: Beyond Large Caps: Exploring The S&P MidCap 400 And S&P SmallCap 600
data.description:
The S&P Composite 1500® serves as a benchmark for the U.S. equity market, aggregating the performance of large caps, mid caps and small caps. While the S&P 500® has global relevance, there is a universe beyond the S&P 500 that market participants sometimes overlook. The mid- and small-cap segments, as measured by the S&P MidCap 400® and S&P SmallCap 600®, offer distinct views in terms of exposure, sensitivity, diversification and return drivers.

Compared to The 500™, the S&P 400® and S&P 600® indices have different sector weights. As of Jan. 31, 2025, they were primarily underweight in Information Technology and Communication Services, while having greater weight in Industrials, Financials, Real Estate and Materials. Although weights vary across the size spectrum, the mid- and small-cap segments had higher weight in domestically focused sectors.

Additionally, the mid- and small-cap segments tend to be more diversified than their large-cap counterpart, which could be relevant for market participants looking to diversify away from large names. Using the adjusted HHI1 to measure concentration, Exhibit 2 shows that the S&P 400 and S&P 600 were less concentrated than The 500.

Moving down the size spectrum presents both opportunities and challenges, resulting in different risk/return profiles. As shown in Exhibit 3, the S&P 400 and S&P 600 outperformed the S&P 500 over various periods but, not surprisingly, exhibited more volatility.

The S&P Composite 1500 is constructed with an earnings screen that requires consistent profitability for its new constituents. Therefore, in addition to the size tilt, the indices also have a positive tilt toward quality. Moreover, the S&P 400 and S&P 600 also tend to have a significant tilt toward value.

Considering indexing beyond large caps, Exhibit 5 presents data from our SPIVA Scorecards in the mid- and small-cap segments, demonstrating that active managers have found it challenging to outperform the S&P 400 and S&P 600. In fact, the percentage of underperforming funds increased to over 90% for longer time periods.

The S&P 500 is a staple in the U.S. equity market, but the S&P 400 and S&P 600 may offer interesting and distinct characteristics: diversification from mega-cap companies, tilts toward more domestically focused sectors and a reflection of high-growth prospect companies. In addition to these drivers, the indices have historically shown quality and value tilts. Furthermore, most active managers underperformed the indices over different time periods, providing evidence that a low cost, index-based approach can offer outperformance in the mid- and small-cap segments.

1The Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) is a widely used metric for measuring concentration, determined as the sum of the squared percentage weights of the index constituents. The adjusted HHI is calculated by dividing the index HHI by the HHI of an equally weighted portfolio with the same number of stocks.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit S&P Dow Jones Indices. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

data.title: Genuine Parts: Margins Are Guided Down Despite Expected Industrial Recovery
data.description:
Genuine Parts Company (NYSE:GPC), the automotive replacement parts retailer and distributor, reported its Q4 results on the 18th of February, also providing a guidance for 2025. The company's performance has been weakened by weak industry conditions, also contributing to subdued Q4 earnings. On the other hand, the 2025 sales performance in Industrial is now suggested to improve, while the retail outlook remains more uncertain. GPC still expects profitability to decline in 2025, and the EPS guidance range came in notably below Wall Street’s consensus, likely leading to the stock's moderate -4% decline following the Q4 report.

In my previous September 2023 article on the stock, “Genuine Parts: Stable But Lacks Organic Growth,” I initiated GPC at a Hold rating with the company lacking significant organic growth. After the article was published, GPC stock has now lost -20% of its value, compared to the S&P 500’s (SP500) return of 36%. The company has still continued to increase its dividend, now for the 69th consecutive year, providing a current yield of 3.5%.

GPC Q4: Still Subdued But Slightly Above Expectations

While still subdued, GPC’s Q4 results came in very slightly above Wall Street expectations. Sales grew 3.3% to $5.8 billion, $60 million above Wall Street’s consensus; both the Automotive and Industrial segments showed a small improvement from full-year comparable sales growth, while remaining at an overall subdued level. The sales growth did include around a 1.1 percentage point favorable impact from one additional day during the fiscal quarter.

The sales performance was driven by 6.1% sales growth to $3.7 billion in the Automotive segment. This was mainly driven by the acquisition of 181 previously independently run NAPA Auto Stores in the U.S. among other similar acquisitions, as well as good retail expansion in the European, Australian, and Asian markets. Comparable store sales growth was still slow at just 0.2%, but explained by industry-wide weakness. For example, automotive parts retailer AutoZone’s (AZO) comparable sales also only grew 0.4% in the company’s latest quarter. O’Reilly Automotive (ORLY) has also shown weaker comparable sales growth in most 2024 quarters and in the 2025 outlook despite a strong Q4, as I wrote in my recent article on the company; the consumer demand for automotive parts has been quite muted.

In the Industrial segment, GPC’s sales declined -1.2% to $2.1 billion, with a -1.7% decline in comparable sales. The segment’s performance seems not to be as well explained by industry factors – for example, the US ISM manufacturing PMI showed year-on-year improvements in Q4, and US automotive sales increased quite well year-on-year throughout the quarter. Also, Fastenal’s (FAST) daily net sales increased 2.1% in Q4, showing a better performance than GPC – GPC's Industrial segment performance has been slightly disappointing.

GPC’s underlying profitability was also mixed in Q4. Excluding a $62 million one-off charge, the gross margin expanded 50 basis points to 36.9%. However, operating costs increased rapidly, causing profitability to decline – SG&A reached $1.70 billion, up $176 million year-on-year due to weaker segment performances and extraordinarily high corporate expenses – adjusted EBITDA ultimately came in at $434 million, down $89 million year-on-year. The quarter’s adjusted EPS of $1.61 still beat Wall Street’s consensus by $0.06, as weakening profitability was already expected.

The 2025 Guidance Is Stronger, But Below Expectations

After an overall subdued Q4 performance, GPC suggests sales growth to improve slightly in 2025. The company now guides for 2-4% sales growth in both segments, comparing to 3.7% growth in Automotive and a -1.4% decline in Industrial in 2024. The sales guidance came very slightly below Wall Street’s 3.15% revenue growth consensus, as analysts seem to have anticipated a very slightly faster recovery. I believe that the sales guidance is still encouraging, as the guidance for the Industrial segment suggests a good growth recovery after the weaker Q4. In the Automotive segment, O’Reilly has, for example, guided below-average growth for 2025 amid turbulent spending power, also likely contributing to GPC’s consistently slow Automotive segment guidance.

More negatively, the adjusted EPS is guided at $7.75-8.25, comparing to $8.16 in 2024 and Wall Street’s consensus of $8.29 – GPC’s margins are guided to continue to decline in 2025 despite stronger expected sales, and for earnings to even decline; it seems that GPC’s operating expenses will continue to increase to support revenues, and that previous years' profitability level isn't completely sustainable. The decline in profitability comes despite restructuring that is expected to provide $100-125 million in savings in 2025.

Updated Valuation

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model on GPC considerably to update the fair value estimate. I now estimate adjusted EBITDA profitability instead of EBIT, and estimate GPC to continue acquisitions at a slow pace going forward, now factored into cash flow and earnings growth.

With a contribution from very slow organic growth and a slight boost from acquisitions, I estimate GPC’s revenues to grow at a 2.5% CAGR through the 2024-2034 period, slightly above previous growth expectations due to the DCF model now factoring in acquisitions. After 2034, I estimate perpetual growth of 2%.

As GPC’s profitability has declined in recent quarters, and is guided to continue to moderately decline in 2025 after the company achieved very good profitability in the 2022-2023 period, I estimate the adjusted EBITDA margin to ultimately stabilize at 8.0%. The estimate is down 50 basis points from 8.5% in 2024, but quite in line with GPC’s long-term history.

The expected acquisitions and GPC’s guided elevated capital expenditures are likely to worsen the cash flow conversion in upcoming years. In addition, the conversion from adjusted EBITDA is worsened by taxes and additional GAAP expenses. Overall, I still expect cash flow to remain at quite a healthy level even after acquisitions.

The estimates put GPC’s fair value at $123.06, near the stock price at the time of writing. I believe that the valuation doesn’t provide an asymmetric risk-to-reward in either direction, as the market now already prices in quite slow organic earnings growth going forward.

The fair value estimate is up slightly from $119.43 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 7.09%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 2.74% interest rate, annualized from the most recent quarter’s interest expenses and debt. The amount of debt remains moderate, and I estimate a long-term debt-to-equity ratio of 20%.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.52% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.33% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. Seeking Alpha now estimates GPC’s beta at 0.78. With a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity stands at 8.10% and the WACC at 7.09%.

Takeaway

GPC's Q4 performance was subdued, as Wall Street already expected. While the Automotive segment's performance seems to be explained by industry-wide factors, the Industrial segment was weak despite some encouraging economic signals. Profitability also slightly declined in Q4, as a higher adjusted gross margin was more than offset by rapid growth in operating expenses.

The 2025 sales guidance seems more positive in the Industrial segment, while Automotive sales are guided to grow at a similar rate as in 2024, suggesting a slow organic performance due to continued slow industry growth. Negatively, GPC guides profitability to continue to decline in 2025 against Wall Street's expectations, contributing to even negative earnings growth in the year despite stronger expected Industrial sales.

I estimate Genuine Parts Company stock to value the company's earnings potential correctly, and as such, I remain with a Hold rating for GPC.

Historical performance for the R5 Class prior to its inception is based on the performance of I Class shares, which have the same expenses as the R5 Class.

Expense ratio is as of the fund's current prospectus. The I Class minimum investment amount is$5 million($3 million for endowments and foundations) per fund. The R5 Share Class is available only to participants in group employer-sponsored retirement plans where a financial intermediary provides recordkeeping services to plan participants.

Periods greater than one year have been annualized.

Portfolio Review

Equities gained during the period. U.S. equities broadly advanced during the quarter, despite a pullback in December. The rally included mid-cap stocks after the overhang of the U.S. elections lifted in November. Inflation remained stubborn, and investors have been rethinking their assumptions about 2025, given that the Federal Reserve is likely to enact fewer interest rate cuts than they previously anticipated.

Growth stocks outperformed. Growth stocks outpaced their value counterparts during the quarter, even as some investors sought lower volatility positions. Consumer discretionary, communication services and financials advanced during the quarter, while real estate, health care and materials declined.

Consumer staples detracted. Consumer staples slumped during the period, and the portfolio’s overweight position in the sector relative to the benchmark weighed on performance. Weak volumes in the beverages industry slowed performance for portfolio holdings like Heineken (OTCQX:HEINY)(OTCQX:HINKF) and Pernod Ricard (OTCPK:PDRDF)(OTCPK:PRNDY).

Communication services slowed results. The portfolio’s light exposure to the communication services sector relative to the benchmark hindered performance. Not owning any benchmark names in the entertainment industry dampened results.

Materials helped. Stock selections in the materials sector gave performance a lift. Materials was among the weakest-performing sectors during the period. Avoiding stocks in the chemicals and metals and mining industries buoyed results.

Top 10 Holdings (%)

Zimmer Biomet Holdings Inc (ZBH) 2.97
Enterprise Products Partners LP (EPD) 2
Henry Schein Inc (HSIC) 1.98
Conagra Brands Inc 1.92
Edison International (EIX) 1.81
US Bancorp 1.76
Truist Financial Corp (TFC) 1.73
Willis Towers Watson PLC (WTW) 1.71
Norfolk Southern Corp (NSC) 1.63
Northern Trust Corp (NTRS) 1.63

As of 12/31/2024

The holdings listed should not be considered recommendations to purchase or sell a particular security. Equity holdings are grouped to include common shares, depository receipts, rights and warrants issued by the same company. Fund holdings subject to change.

Key Contributors

Northern Trust (NTRS). Shares of this financial services firm advanced during the period as positive pricing and deposit trends resulted in improving net interest income. In addition, Northern Trust’s fee income was broadly up year over year.

Emerson Electric (EMR). The electric products company delivered what we viewed as strong margins during the period and realized higher-than-expected cost synergies from buying National Instruments. The market also welcomed Emerson’s decision to buy the remaining shares and take full ownership of Aspen Technology, an industrial software provider.

Beacon Roofing Supply (BECN). Shares of this distributor of residential and business roofing supplies outperformed during the period as reports suggested Beacon could be a buyout target of a holding company that is looking to buy similar building product distributors.

Key Detractors

Pernod Ricard. This France-based maker of wines and spirits has underperformed like many of its peers in the beverage industry as volumes have been weak, particularly in the U.S.

Conagra Brands (CAG). Shares of this packaged-food products company have seen persistently weak volumes as consumers look for deals. Conagra cut profit guidance but reported better-than-expected organic growth.

MicroStrategy (MSTR). Lack of exposure to this software company hindered performance. MicroStrategy issues equity to buy bitcoin, and surging cryptocurrency prices are a primary driver of the company’s stock performance. We believe neither MicroStrategy’s software business nor its bitcoin holdings meet our investment criteria.

Notable Trades

The Estee Lauder Cos (EL). We initiated a position in this beauty and cosmetics company after an extended period of underperformance resulted in what we believed was an attractive entry point for its shares. We think its business can stabilize and its earnings can improve.

American Tower (AMT). We initiated a position in this telecommunications real estate investment trust on what we believed was weakness after the sale of its India business resulted in earnings dilution. In our view, American Tower’s shares trade at an attractive entry point, and the company offers a favorable risk/reward profile.

BCE (BCE). We exited our position in this Canada-based telecommunications company after it took on debt to buy a U.S.-based fiber internet provider. We expected BCE to take proceeds generated from the sale of its stakes in professional sports teams to pay down debt rather than add to it with another acquisition.

Darden Restaurants (DRI). We exited our position in this restaurant operator after the stock reached a new all-time high. In turn, we funded other investments that we believe offered better risk/reward profiles.

Portfolio Positioning

The portfolio seeks to invest in companies where we believe the valuation does not reflect the quality and normal earnings power of the company. Our process is based on individual security selection, but broad themes have emerged.

A focus on what we believe are higher-quality, durable stocks. Central banks started cutting interest rates as inflation continued to moderate, but we continue to believe the economy presents an uneven landscape for investors. Continuing geopolitical risks have played an additional factor in navigating the markets. In this environment, and in others, we remain focused on companies that we think are of higher quality because they generate stable revenues and profits and have low debt, steady cash flows and predictable business models that can offer resilience in a variety of economic conditions.

Attractive valuations in health care. Our research has led us to several health care stocks that we think offer compelling valuations and risk/reward profiles. We consider health care less cyclical because demand in the sector is typically less impacted by the economy’s performance. Therefore, in a slowing economy, we think patients seeking elective procedures should provide support to medical device companies and service providers that continue to work through patient backlogs caused by the COVID-19 disruption.

Opportunities in consumer staples. With slowing global growth, we have identified select opportunities in the relatively defensive consumer staples sector. Despite a challenging cost inflation environment, our research indicated many consumer staples companies have been generating strong returns on capital, buying back stock and growing dividends. Moreover, industry consolidation has enabled companies to pass higher costs to consumers. We believe actions to offset inflation, including fewer discounts and more price hikes, should support earnings and margins.

Shifting outlook for the financials sector. After a period of turmoil for regional banks following the failure of a few banks in 2023, we believe the outlook for the industry could improve. We expect banks will face a less strenuous regulatory backdrop, including greater openness to mergers and acquisitions. We also think net interest income should improve from ongoing repricing and a potential uplift in loan demand. Insurance companies have improved their pricing power, particularly for property policies, due to easing regulations on rates and fewer competitors in disaster-prone markets.

Limited opportunities in consumer discretionary. Our portfolio remains underweight in the consumer discretionary sector relative to the benchmark. We believe this sector has fewer high-quality companies with durable business models. Also, given factors like inflation and diminishing personal savings, we expect there will be headwinds to discretionary spending.

Historically, small- and/or mid-cap stocks have been more volatile than the stocks of larger, more established companies. Smaller companies may have limited resources, product lines and markets, and their securities may trade less frequently and in more limited volumes than those of larger companies.

The Russell Midcap® Index measures the performance of the 800 smallest companies in the Russell 1000® Index. The Russell Midcap® Value Index measures the performance of those Russell Midcap® companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower forecasted growth values. The stocks are also members of the Russell 1000® Value Index. Created by Frank Russell Company, indices are not investment products available for purchase.

data.title: Franklin Electric Co., Inc. 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Franklin Electric Co., Inc. in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.
